skill.name: browser-automation
skill.description: Guidance for using rust-browser-mcp to automate Chrome, Firefox, and Edge through WebDriver. It covers navigation, form automation, scraping, screenshots, performance checks, sessions, and reusable recipes.
skill.summary: Automate browser workflows, testing, scraping, screenshots, and performance checks with WebDriver.
skill.author: EmilLindfors

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution and weak-crypto patterns, but most are false positives from markdown tool names, JSON examples, and ordinary documentation text. Confirmed risks are legitimate but elevated: the skill enables arbitrary browser navigation, browser-context JavaScript execution, credential-based login workflows, screenshots, and setup scripts that can build software or write Claude Desktop configuration. No prompt injection attempt, credential exfiltration, or malicious intent was found in the reviewed files.

medium_findings.title: Arbitrary Browser Navigation and JavaScript Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill is intended to automate browsers against user-provided sites and documents execute_script for browser-context JavaScript. This is legitimate for testing and scraping, but it can access page content, trigger site actions, and extract protected data if used without authorization.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented execute_script and scraping workflows directly confirm browser-context script execution and extraction. The capability is expected for this skill, so the risk depends on user authorization and target site rules.

medium_findings.title: Credential-Based Login Automation
medium_findings.description: The documentation includes login_form usage and recipe examples with username and password parameters. The skill warns not to store credentials in recipes, but users could still place secrets into reusable workflows or automated scraping sessions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The login examples explicitly include password fields and recipe parameters. The presence of a security best practice lowers concern but does not remove the handling risk.

medium_findings.title: Setup Scripts Modify Local Configuration and Build Software
medium_findings.description: The shell setup script can run cargo build, and the Python helper can create or overwrite the Claude Desktop MCP configuration. These are normal setup tasks, but marketplace users should review scripts before execution because they affect local files and installed tooling.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scripts clearly perform a local build and write a configuration file. I found no malicious destination or exfiltration behavior, so this is an operational risk rather than a blocker.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Static External Command Flags in Markdown
low_findings.description: Most external command findings point to backticked MCP tool names or JSON examples in markdown, not shell or Ruby command execution. These are documentation examples for browser automation tools.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are markdown lists and JSON examples, not executable code paths. This strongly supports a false positive classification for the bulk markdown findings.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Examples and Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs reviewed are example targets or installer documentation links. No evidence found that the skill sends data to an unauthorized remote endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL contexts are visibly examples or dependency download references. I did not find evidence of hidden network exfiltration in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Temporary Screenshot Paths Are User-Controlled Output Examples
low_findings.description: Several filesystem findings reference /tmp screenshot output paths in examples. This can expose captured page content if screenshots include sensitive data, but it is normal browser automation behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations explicitly describe screenshot paths rather than arbitrary file reads. The residual concern is privacy of screenshot contents, not malicious filesystem access.

dangerous_patterns.title: Browser-Context JavaScript Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The execute_script tool runs JavaScript in the active page context and should be limited to authorized pages and test environments.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The tool is explicitly documented and examples use it for DOM extraction. This is a known high-power browser automation primitive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Automated Login and Secret Input
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents login automation with password fields and recipe parameters, which can mishandle secrets if stored or logged.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples directly include password placeholders and protected-content scraping. The risk is contextual but real for user secrets and access controls.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Configuration File Write
dangerous_patterns.description: The configuration helper writes MCP server settings into the Claude Desktop config path for the current platform.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The helper explicitly opens the config path for writing and dumps JSON. This is expected setup behavior but should be disclosed to users.

content.user_title: Automate Browser Testing and Web Workflows
content.value_statement: Manual browser checks, scraping tasks, and performance reviews are slow and hard to repeat. This skill provides structured WebDriver workflows for automated navigation, forms, screenshots, scraping, and browser health checks.
actual_capabilities: Documents browser navigation, element selection, clicking, typing, hovering, scrolling, and page status checks.
Shows form filling, login workflows, multi-step forms, validation checks, and CAPTCHA boundaries.
Provides web scraping patterns for product lists, paginated pages, infinite scroll, tables, and protected content.
Covers screenshots, window resizing, current URL checks, page source access, and DOM data extraction.
Explains performance metrics, memory monitoring, console log review, resource monitoring, and repeated performance tests.
Includes reusable recipe patterns and setup helpers for Claude Desktop MCP configuration.
limitations: Requires the rust-browser-mcp server, a supported browser, and a matching WebDriver installation.
Browser automation must follow target site terms, access controls, and rate limits.
JavaScript execution and screenshots can expose sensitive page data if used on authenticated pages.
The setup scripts affect local configuration and should be reviewed before running.

use_cases.title: Regression Browser Checks
use_cases.description: Automate repeatable navigation, form submission, screenshots, and console log checks across Chrome, Firefox, or Edge.

use_cases.title: Structured Data Collection
use_cases.description: Extract page text, tables, links, and DOM values from authorized pages with reusable scraping workflows.

use_cases.title: Performance Review Workflows
use_cases.description: Run page load checks, collect memory metrics, monitor resources, and compare page versions over repeated iterations.

prompt_templates.title: Open and Inspect a Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the browser automation skill to open [URL], wait for the main content, report the page title, and summarize visible issues.

prompt_templates.title: Test a Form Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Automate the form at [URL] with these test values: [VALUES]. Check validation messages, submit the form, and capture the final state.

prompt_templates.title: Extract Authorized Page Data
prompt_templates.prompt: Scrape the authorized page at [URL]. Extract [FIELDS], handle pagination if present, and return a concise table of results and gaps.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Performance Recipe
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a reusable recipe that tests [URL] in Chrome and Firefox, records load metrics, checks console errors, and saves screenshots for failures.

output_examples.input: Check a staging login form with test credentials and report whether validation works.
output: The form loaded and accepted valid test input.
Invalid email text produced the expected validation message.
The password field did not expose entered text.
A screenshot was captured after the failed validation state.

output_examples.input: Collect product names and prices from an authorized product listing page.
output: Extracted 24 product entries across three pages.
Two products were missing prices and were flagged for review.
No login or protected data was accessed during the run.

output_examples.input: Compare performance for two versions of a marketing page.
output: Version B loaded faster on average across three runs.
Version A had more console warnings and larger image resources.
Both versions completed rendering without detected JavaScript errors.

best_practices: Use only authorized URLs and test accounts, especially for login and scraping workflows.
Keep credentials out of saved recipes, screenshots, logs, and shared prompt history.
Review setup scripts and generated Claude Desktop configuration before applying them.
anti_patterns: Do not use the skill to bypass CAPTCHAs, paywalls, access controls, or site rate limits.
Do not run JavaScript extraction on pages that contain secrets unless the data handling plan is clear.
Do not store production passwords or session tokens in reusable recipes.

faq.question: What browsers does this skill support?
faq.answer: The documentation covers Chrome, Firefox, and Edge through WebDriver-compatible drivers.

faq.question: Does it include the browser automation server?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and setup helpers for using the rust-browser-mcp server with installed WebDrivers.

faq.question: Can it scrape any website?
faq.answer: It should only be used on sites where you have authorization and where automation follows the site rules.

faq.question: Can it handle authenticated workflows?
faq.answer: Yes, the examples cover login forms, but credentials should use test accounts and should not be saved in recipes.

faq.question: Can it measure page performance?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill documents page timing metrics, memory monitoring, console logs, resource usage, and repeated performance tests.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill has powerful browser, script, credential, and local setup capabilities, but no malicious intent was found.
